title: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders

items.subtopic: Tremor, Chorea, and Other Movement Disorders | HYPERKINETIC MOVEMENT DISORDERS | CLINICAL FEATURES
items.title: Characteristics of Essential Tremor (ET) vs. Parkinson's Disease (PD)
items.content: Essential tremor is characterized by postural or action tremors that occur when maintaining a posture or reaching for a target, respectively. In contrast, Parkinson's disease typically presents with a resting tremor, which diminishs during voluntary movement.
keyPoints: ET: Postural and/or action tremor
PD: Predominant resting tremor
Cerebellar dysfunction: Kinetic tremor associated with hypotonia and past pointing
Physiologic tremor: Mild, high-frequency (10–12 Hz), postural or action tremor in upper extremities
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · Tremor, Chorea, and Other Movement Disorders | HYPERKINETIC MOVEMENT DISORDERS | CLINICAL FEATURES

items.subtopic: Tremor, Chorea, and Other Movement Disorders | HYPERKINETIC MOVEMENT DISORDERS | CLINICAL FEATURES
items.title: Enhanced Physiologic Tremor (EPT) and Its Management
items.content: Enhanced physiologic tremor is a mild, high-frequency tremor that can be exacerbated by stressors such as lifting weights, anxiety, fatigue, metabolic disturbances, or certain substances. It affects up to 10% of the population.
keyPoints: EPT: Mild, high-frequency (10–12 Hz) tremor
Prevalence: Up to 10% of the population
Triggers: Weight lifting, anxiety, fatigue, hyperthyroidism, electrolyte abnormalities, drugs/toxins like valproate, lithium, caffeine
Management: Control underlying disorder; beta blockers may be used if necessary
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · Tremor, Chorea, and Other Movement Disorders | HYPERKINETIC MOVEMENT DISORDERS | CLINICAL FEATURES

items.title: Prevalence and Demographics of Essential Tremor (ET)
items.content: Essential Tremor is the most common movement disorder, affecting approximately 1% of the general population. Its prevalence increases with age, impacting about 5% of individuals over 60 years old. ET can manifest in childhood but becomes more prevalent in those older than 70 years.
keyPoints: Affects ~1% of the population
Impacts ~5% of those over 60 years
Prevalence increases dramatically in those aged >70 years
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · ESSENTIAL TREMOR

items.title: Characteristics and Clinical Features of Essential Tremor (ET)
items.content: Essential Tremor is characterized by a high-frequency tremor, typically 6–10 Hz, affecting the upper extremities. It often presents as postural or action tremor and can interfere with daily activities like eating and drinking. ET is usually bilateral and symmetric but may start asymmetrically. The tremor can involve other body parts such as the head (~30%), voice (~20%), tongue (~20%), face/jaw (~10%), and lower limbs (~10%). It is improved by alcohol consumption and worsened by stress.
keyPoints: High-frequency tremor (6–10 Hz)
Primarily affects upper extremities
Bilateral and symmetric, may be asymmetric initially
Involves head in ~30%, voice in ~20%, tongue in ~20%, face/jaw in ~10%, lower limbs in ~10%
Improved by alcohol, worsened by stress
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · ESSENTIAL TREMOR

items.title: Understanding the Genetic and Pathophysiological Basis of Essential Tremor (ET)
items.content: Essential tremor's etiology involves a complex interplay of genetic factors, with approximately 50% of patients having a positive family history following an autosomal dominant pattern. The NOTCH2NLC gene has been implicated due to GGC repeat expansions in some cases, though no causative gene is confirmed. Pathophysiologically, the cerebellum and inferior olives are considered potential sites for altered tremor pacemaking, supported by findings of increased metabolic activity and blood flow in these regions.
keyPoints: Approximately 50% of ET patients have a positive family history with an autosomal dominant pattern.
Linkage studies suggest possible loci; NOTCH2NLC gene GGC repeat expansion is associated but not confirmed as causative.
Cerebellum and inferior olives implicated in tremor pacemaking, supported by increased metabolic activity and blood flow.
Pathologic findings of cerebellar pathology (loss of Purkinje cells) are controversial.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · ETIOLOGY AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY | TREATMENT

items.title: Effective Management Strategies for Essential Tremor (ET)
items.content: Treatment for ET varies based on severity, with many cases requiring only reassurance. For more severe tremors impacting daily activities, beta blockers like propranolol and primidone are standard therapies, effective in approximately 50% of patients. Propranolol is dosed between 20–120 mg daily, while primidone starts at low doses to minimize side effects. Alternatives include gabapentin and topiramate, with botulinum toxin for limb or voice tremor, though it may cause muscle weakness. For severe, drug-resistant cases, surgical targeting of the VIM nucleus or focal ultrasound therapy are effective options.
keyPoints: Many ET cases do not require treatment beyond reassurance.
Beta blockers (propranolol 20–120 mg daily) and primidone ($\sim$50% efficacy) are standard treatments.
Propranolol is contraindicated in bradycardia or asthma; primidone should be started at low doses to avoid side effects.
Botulinum toxin can help with limb/voice tremor but may cause muscle weakness.
Surgical targeting of the VIM nucleus and focal ultrasound are options for severe, drug-resistant ET.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · ETIOLOGY AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY | TREATMENT

items.title: Distinguishing Dystonic Tremor from Essential Tremor (ET)
items.content: Dystonic tremor can be differentiated from essential tremor by its unique characteristics: it is most pronounced when the affected body part moves in the direction of dystonia and is relieved when moved in the opposite direction.
keyPoints: Dystonic tremor is most pronounced with movement in the direction of dystonia
Tremor relief occurs when moving in the opposite direction to dystonia
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · CLINICAL FEATURES

items.title: Classification and Prevalence of Dystonia
items.content: Dystonia is classified based on clinical features such as age of onset, body distribution, temporal pattern, and additional features. It has a frequency estimated at about 30 per 100,000 but may be underdiagnosed.
keyPoints: Frequency of dystonia: ~30 per 100,000
Classification axes: clinical (age of onset, body distribution, etc.) and etiologic (genetic abnormalities)
Over 200 genes linked to different types of dystonia
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · CLINICAL FEATURES

items.title: Prevalence and Clinical Presentation of Focal Dystonia
items.content: Focal dystonia is the most common form of isolated dystonia, typically presenting in adults between their fourth to sixth decade. Women are affected twice as often as men, except for writer's cramp, which is more prevalent in men.
keyPoints: Most frequent form of isolated dystonia
Typically presents in the fourth to sixth decade
Women affected about twice as often as men (except for writer's cramp)
~40% cervical dystonia, ~15% blepharospasm
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · ISOLATED DYSTONIAS (part 1)

items.title: Characteristics and Etiology of Focal Dystonias
items.content: Focal dystonias can extend to other body regions in ~30% of cases, often misdiagnosed as psychiatric or orthopedic. Causes are usually unknown; they are rarely monogenic (~1%), with autoimmunity and trauma suggested as possible etiologies.
keyPoints: ~30% of focal dystonias extend to other body regions
Often misdiagnosed as psychiatric or orthopedic in origin
Rarely monogenic (~1%)
Autoimmunity and trauma are suggested etiologies
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · ISOLATED DYSTONIAS (part 1)

items.title: Genetic Basis of Generalized Dystonia
items.content: Generalized dystonia often has a hereditary basis with onset in childhood or adolescence. The TOR1A gene mutation is the most common cause, especially among Ashkenazi Jewish patients due to a founder effect.
keyPoints: Often hereditary with onset in childhood or adolescence
TOR1A gene mutation is the most common cause of early-onset generalized dystonia
3-base pair deletion in TOR1A gene
Founder effect among Ashkenazi Jewish patients
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · ISOLATED DYSTONIAS (part 1)

items.title: ANO3 Gene Mutations and Their Role in Craniocervical Dystonia
items.content: Mutations in the ANO3 gene are associated with craniocervical dystonia, confirmed by familial cases showing dystonia and myoclonic jerks. While missense variants exist in healthy individuals, their pathogenic role is validated through additional family descriptions.
keyPoints: ANO3 mutations linked to craniocervical dystonia
Pathogenic role confirmed by familial cases with dystonia and myoclonic jerks
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · ISOLATED DYSTONIAS (part 2)

items.title: EIF2AK2 Variants and Early-Onset Limb Dystonia
items.content: Pathogenic missense variants in EIF2AK2, particularly p.Gly130Arg, cause dystonia with a median onset age of 6 years, often beginning in the limbs. This gene is linked to eIF2a phosphorylation pathways.
keyPoints: EIF2AK2 mutations cause early-onset limb dystonia
Median age at onset: 6 years
Recurrent variant: p.Gly130Arg
Linked to eIF2a phosphorylation pathway
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · ISOLATED DYSTONIAS (part 2)

items.title: Recognizing Dopa-responsive Dystonia (DRD) for Effective Treatment
items.content: Dopa-responsive dystonia, also known as Segawa syndrome, is caused by mutations in the GCH1 gene. It manifests with childhood-onset dystonia that shows diurnal fluctuations and responds dramatically to low doses of levodopa.
keyPoints: Mutations in the GCH1 gene cause DRD.
Symptoms include childhood-onset dystonia with diurnal fluctuations.
Low doses of levodopa lead to dramatic response.
>100 different mutations reported, penetrance ~50%.
Higher incidence in women compared to men.
Recessive inheritance results in more severe phenotype with developmental delay and infantile onset.
Lifelong response to levodopa without developing dyskinesia due to preserved dopamine neuronal network.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · COMBINED DYSTONIAS (part 1)

items.title: Understanding X-linked Dystonia-Parkinsonism in Filipino Populations
items.content: X-linked dystonia-parkinsonism, or Lubag, is an exclusively Filipino disorder caused by a retrotransposon insertion in the TAF1 gene. It presents with focal dystonia that generalizes and transitions to L-dopa-unresponsive parkinsonism.
keyPoints: Exclusive to patients of Filipino origin due to founder effect.
Caused by retrotransposon insertion in TAF1 gene.
Initial presentation is focal dystonia, progressing to parkinsonism over 5-10 years.
L-dopa-unresponsive parkinsonism develops later.
Age-at-onset variability explained by hexameric repeat expansion and SNPs in MSH3 and PMS1 genes.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · COMBINED DYSTONIAS (part 1)

items.title: Identifying Myoclonic-Dystonia Linked to SGCE Gene Mutations
items.content: Myoclonic-dystonia is characterized by action-induced myoclonic jerks and psychiatric features, often linked to mutations in the SGCE gene. These mutations are maternally imprinted, manifesting only when inherited from the father.
keyPoints: Characterized by action-induced, alcohol-responsive myoclonic jerks.
Onset usually in childhood or adolescence.
Psychiatric features include depression and anxiety-related disorders.
Linked to mutations in the SGCE gene.
~80 different mutations reported, including deletions of the entire gene.
Mutations manifest only when inherited from the father due to maternal imprinting.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · COMBINED DYSTONIAS (part 1)

items.title: Understanding the Etiology of Combined Dystonias
items.content: Combined dystonias often form part of a complex syndrome with multiple clinical manifestations. They are frequently hereditary, such as in Wilson's disease or Lesch-Nyhan syndrome, but can also result from drugs/toxins or brain lesions.
keyPoints: Commonly hereditary: Wilson's disease, Lesch-Nyhan syndrome
Drug-induced dystonia often seen with neuroleptic drugs and chronic levodopa treatment in PD patients

Can develop due to discrete lesions in the striatum, globus pallidus, thalamus, cortex, or brainstem from causes like infarction, hemorrhage, anoxia, trauma, tumor, infection, or toxins (e.g., manganese, carbon monoxide)
Dystonia may be segmental if lesions are unilateral; generalized if bilateral/widespread
Rarely follows peripheral nerve injury with complex regional pain syndrome features
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · COMBINED DYSTONIAS (part 2) | PATHOPHYSIOLOGY OF DYSTONIA

items.title: Pathophysiology of Dystonia: Basal Ganglia and Dopamine System Involvement
items.content: The pathophysiology involves abnormal muscle recruitment due to deranged action selection, with loss of surround inhibition at multiple motor system levels. The basal ganglia are central in dystonia's origin, with dopamine playing a key role.
keyPoints: Dystonia characterized by co-contracting synchronous bursts of agonist and antagonist muscles
Abnormal recruitment of inappropriate muscles for given actions due to loss of surround inhibition at multiple motor system levels (cortex, brainstem, spinal cord)
Increased cortical excitability and reorganization observed
Basal ganglia alterations in blood flow/metabolism implicated; lesions can induce dystonia
Surgical interventions like ablation or DBS of globus pallidus may ameliorate dystonia
Dopamine system involved as dopaminergic therapies can both induce and treat some forms of dystonia
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · COMBINED DYSTONIAS (part 2) | PATHOPHYSIOLOGY OF DYSTONIA

items.title: Management of Acute Dystonic Reactions
items.content: Acute dystonia should be managed by immediately discontinuing any precipitating agents and administering medications such as diphenhydramine, benztropine, benzodiazepines, or dopamine agonists.
keyPoints: Immediate withdrawal of precipitating agent is crucial.
Use diphenhydramine, benztropine, benzodiazepines, or dopamine agonists for treatment.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · Dystonia

items.title: Treatment Approaches for Chronic Dystonia
items.content: Chronic dystonia is primarily managed symptomatically. Levodopa testing is recommended in childhood-onset cases to check for dopa-responsive dystonia (DRD). High-dose anticholinergics and oral baclofen may be used, but intrathecal baclofen can offer more benefits despite potential complications.
keyPoints: Symptomatic treatment is the mainstay for chronic dystonia.
Levodopa should be tried in childhood-onset dystonia to test for DRD.
High-dose anticholinergics (e.g., trihexyphenidyl 20–120 mg/d) may benefit children but have cognitive side effects in adults.
Oral baclofen (20–120 mg) has modest benefits and potential sedation, weakness, memory loss.
Intrathecal baclofen is more effective for leg and trunk dystonia but carries risks of infection, seizures, coma.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · Dystonia

items.title: Botulinum Toxin and Surgical Interventions in Dystonia
items.content: Botulinum toxin is preferred for focal and segmental dystonia, acting by blocking acetylcholine release. For severe generalized dystonia unresponsive to other treatments, deep brain stimulation (DBS) of the pallidum can be effective.
keyPoints: Botulinum toxin blocks acetylcholine at neuromuscular junctions for focal/segmental dystonia.
Repeat injections every 2-4 months; long-acting formulation approved for cervical dystonia (up to 6 months).
DBS of the pallidum is effective in severe generalized dystonia, especially in younger patients with shorter disease duration and certain monogenic forms like DYT-Tor1A.
Adverse effects of DBS include paresthesia, dysarthria, gait disturbance, mood change; bradykinesia with GPi stimulation.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · Dystonia

items.title: Diagnosis and Confirmation of Huntington's Disease
items.content: A clinical diagnosis of HD can be strongly suspected in cases presenting with chorea and a positive family history. However, genetic testing is required for definitive confirmation.
keyPoints: HD is an autosomal dominant disorder characterized by motor, behavioral, oculomotor, and cognitive dysfunction.
Onset typically occurs between ages 25-45 years.
Genetic testing confirms diagnosis; should be performed with trained counselors due to potential psychological impacts.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · HUNTINGTON'S DISEASE

items.title: Imaging and Pathology in Huntington's Disease
items.content: MRI can reveal progressive atrophy of the head of the caudate nucleus, with possible severe involvement of the putamen. Reduced metabolic activity in these regions supports diagnosis.
keyPoints: Progressive atrophy visible on MRI primarily affects the striatum and cerebral cortex.
Supportive studies include reduced metabolic activity in the caudate nucleus and putamen.
Neuropathology includes neuronal loss, gliosis, and intraneuronal inclusions containing ubiquitin and mutant huntingtin.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · HUNTINGTON'S DISEASE

items.title: Early Detection and Progression Monitoring of Huntington's Disease
items.content: Subtle motor impairments, cognitive alterations, and imaging changes can be detected in at-risk individuals before the full manifestation of HD. This is crucial for future neuroprotective therapies.
keyPoints: HD onset typically between ages 25-45 years; prevalence is 2–8 cases per 100,000.
Early signs include slowed saccades and impaired smooth pursuit movements.
Defining progression rates of early features is essential for studies on disease-modifying therapies.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · HUNTINGTON'S DISEASE

items.title: Genetic Basis and Pathophysiology of Huntington's Disease (HD)
items.content: Huntington's Disease is caused by a CAG repeat expansion in the Huntingtin gene on chromosome 4. The number of repeats (>40) correlates with disease onset, while intermediate forms (36-39 repeats) show reduced penetrance. Anticipation occurs as repeat length increases across generations, particularly in males.
keyPoints: CAG repeats >40 cause HD
Intermediate forms have 36–39 repeats with less severe involvement and reduced penetrance
Anticipation seen in subsequent generations, especially males
Postnatal somatic gene expansion and genetic modifiers (e.g., FAN1, MSH3) affect disease progression
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · ETIOLOGY

items.title: Mechanisms of Neurotoxicity and Therapeutic Implications in HD
items.content: Mutant HTT disrupts transcription, immune function, and mitochondrial activity. Toxic fragments may translocate to the nucleus, affecting protein regulation. Neuronal inclusions might serve as a protective mechanism. Animal models using mutant gene expression or excitotoxic agents help assess therapies.
keyPoints: Mutant HTT RNA is toxic, disrupting various cellular functions
Neuronal inclusions may protect by segregating toxic proteins
Animal models with striatal pathology aid in therapeutic assessment
Early correction of neurotransmitter deficits can prevent disease development in HD mice
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · ETIOLOGY

items.title: Treatment of Chorea in Huntington's Disease
items.content: Current treatment for choreiform movements in Huntington's disease involves dopamine-blocking agents like tetrabenazine and its deuterated form, which have fewer dose-related side effects. Deuteration reduces the maximum concentration (Cmax) to mitigate adverse events such as parkinsonism.
keyPoints: Dopamine-blocking agents approved for chorea include tetrabenazine and valbenazine.
Deuterated tetrabenazine has fewer dose-related side effects and less parkinsonism than non-deuterated forms.
Clinical trials show deuterated tetrabenazine can be administered in higher doses with potentially superior clinical benefits.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · Huntington's Disease (part 1)

items.title: Psychiatric Management in Huntington's Disease
items.content: Depression, anxiety, and psychosis are significant concerns in Huntington's disease. Treatment involves antidepressants, antianxiety drugs, and atypical antipsychotics like clozapine, quetiapine, and risperidone, with doses ranging from 50–600 mg/d for the former two and 2–8 mg/d for risperidone.
keyPoints: Depression and anxiety should be treated with appropriate medications.
Psychosis can be managed with atypical antipsychotics such as clozapine (50–600 mg/d), quetiapine (50–600 mg/d), and risperidone (2–8 mg/d).
Monitor patients for mania and suicidal ideations.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · Huntington's Disease (part 1)

items.title: Experimental Therapies in Huntington's Disease
items.content: Research is ongoing to develop neuroprotective therapies that slow or stop disease progression. Strategies include reducing mutant HTT formation, enhancing mitochondrial function, and using CRISPR gene editing techniques.
keyPoints: No current therapies approved for the disabling motor features of HD.
Experimental approaches focus on inhibiting mRNA synthesis and promoting early mRNA degradation (e.g., antisense oligonucleotides).
CRISPR techniques are being explored to target mutant Huntingtin RNA.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · Huntington's Disease (part 1)

items.title: Distinguishing HDL-1 from Huntington's Disease
items.content: HDL-1, a prion disease caused by expansion of an octapeptide repeat in PRNP, presents with personality changes in the third or fourth decade followed by chorea and other neurological symptoms. It is crucial to differentiate it from Huntington's Disease due to its distinct pathogenesis.
keyPoints: HDL-1 is a prion disease caused by expansion of an octapeptide repeat in PRNP.
Onset typically occurs in the third or fourth decade with personality changes, followed by chorea, rigidity, myoclonus, ataxia, and epilepsy.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · Huntington's Disease (part 2) | ☐ OTHER CHOREAS

items.title: Identifying HDL-4 as SCA 17
items.content: HDL-4 results from trinucleotide repeat expansion in TBP, identical to spinocerebellar ataxia type 17. It often presents with ataxia rather than chorea and has reduced penetrance associated with certain genetic variants.
keyPoints: HDL-4 is caused by trinucleotide repeats in the TATA box-binding protein (TBP) gene, identical to SCA 17.
Presentation typically involves ataxia; some cases have reduced penetrance linked to Stub1 gene variants.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · Huntington's Disease (part 2) | ☐ OTHER CHOREAS

items.title: Recognizing Chorea-Acanthocytosis and Its Genetic Basis
items.content: Chorea-acanthocytosis is an autosomal recessive disorder characterized by chorea and acanthocytes. It involves mutations in the VPS13A gene, with a phenotypically similar X-linked form known as McLeod syndrome.
keyPoints: Characterized by severe chorea and red cell abnormalities (acanthocytes).
Caused by mutations in the VPS13A gene; an X-linked variant is McLeod syndrome.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · Huntington's Disease (part 2) | ☐ OTHER CHOREAS

items.title: Understanding Hemibalismus: Etiology and Management
items.content: Hemibalismus is a severe form of choreiform movement primarily affecting proximal limb muscles on one side. It often results from lesions in the subthalamic nucleus (STN), but can also involve the putamen, thalamus, or parietal cortex. While spontaneous resolution occurs over weeks to months, dopamine-blocking drugs are effective for management. In extreme cases, pallidotomy or deep brain stimulation (DBS) of the globus pallidus interna (GPi) may be necessary.
keyPoints: Hemibalismus involves violent, large-amplitude movements affecting proximal limb muscles on one side.
Common causes include lesions in the STN (60-70%), putamen, thalamus, or parietal cortex.
Dopamine-blocking drugs are effective for management.
Spontaneous resolution occurs over weeks to months.
In extreme cases, pallidotomy or DBS of the GPi can be considered.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · BALLISM/HEMIBALLISMUS | TICS | TOURETTE'S SYNDROME

items.title: Characteristics and Management of Tourette's Syndrome
items.content: Tourette's syndrome (TS) is a neurobehavioral disorder characterized by multiple motor tics and vocalizations. It predominantly affects males, with a prevalence of 0.03–1.6%. Tics can be suppressed voluntarily for short periods but recur due to an irresistible urge. TS often presents between ages 2 and 15 years and may improve in adulthood. Associated conditions include ADHD, OCD, anxiety, and depression. Management involves addressing these comorbidities.
keyPoints: TS is characterized by multiple motor tics and vocalizations.
Prevalence is estimated at 0.03–1.6%, predominantly affecting males.
Tics can be voluntarily suppressed for short periods but recur due to an irresistible urge.
Typically presents between ages 2 and 15 years, often improving in adulthood.
Associated conditions include ADHD, OCD, anxiety, and depression.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · BALLISM/HEMIBALLISMUS | TICS | TOURETTE'S SYNDROME

items.title: Management of Tics in Tourette's Syndrome
items.content: In patients with disabling tics that interfere with quality of life, drug treatment is indicated. Therapy should be individualized due to the lack of robust double-blind trials evaluating many regimens.
keyPoints: Tics often wane in adult life; conservative management is preferred during early decades.
Clonidine and guanfacine ($\alpha$-agonists) are used, with guanfacine preferred for once-daily dosing (0.5–2 mg/d).
Atypical neuroleptics like risperidone, olanzapine, ziprasidone are initial choices due to lower risk of tardive dyskinesia.
If ineffective, classical neuroleptics such as haloperidol, fluphenazine, pimozide, or tiapride may be tried.
Tetrabenazine and deuterated tetrabenazine can cause depression.
Ecopipam (D1 antagonist) showed benefits without serious side effects in a short trial.
Topiramate (antiepileptic drug) may benefit some patients.
Botulinum toxin injections are effective for focal tics involving small muscle groups.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · Tics and Tourette's Syndrome

items.title: Emerging Therapies and Behavioral Management in TS
items.content: Exploration of new therapies like DBS, wrist devices, and addressing behavioral features is ongoing. ADHD medications may exacerbate tics.
keyPoints: Closed-loop DBS targeting the anterior internal capsule, GPi, or thalamus is being explored for severe cases.
A public database and registry for DBS in TS has been established.
Wrist devices delivering electrical pulses have shown promise in reducing tic severity in open-label studies.
Behavioral features like anxiety and compulsions should be treated as they can be disabling.
ADHD medications such as methylphenidate may increase attention but could exacerbate tics.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · Tics and Tourette's Syndrome

items.title: Understanding Myoclonus: Mechanisms and Clinical Implications
items.content: Myoclonus involves brief, rapid muscle jerks that can be focal or generalized. It may occur spontaneously or in response to stimuli, with varying clinical significance. Key mechanisms include abnormal neuronal discharges across various brain regions, often linked to metabolic disturbances, toxins, medications, and genetic factors.
keyPoints: Myoclonus is characterized by brief (<100 ms), shock-like jerks that can be focal or generalized.
It may occur spontaneously, with voluntary movement (action myoclonus), or in response to stimuli (reflex myoclonus).
Associated with abnormal neuronal discharges in cortical, subcortical, brainstem, or spinal cord regions.
Can result from metabolic disturbances, toxins, medications, and genetic factors.
Frequently associated with epilepsy, suggesting a possible epileptic-like discharge mechanism.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · MYOCLONUS

items.title: Diagnostic Approach to Myoclonus: Identifying Underlying Causes
items.content: Diagnosing myoclonus involves identifying its type and underlying cause, which may include metabolic disturbances, genetic disorders, or neurodegenerative diseases. Imaging studies can help define altered neuronal connectivity, while specific syndromes like Unverricht-Lundborg disease and Lafora body epilepsy have distinct genetic causes.
keyPoints: Myoclonic epilepsy is a disorder of myoclonus with epilepsy, often progressive and potentially fatal.
Unverricht-Lundborg disease (EPM-1) involves mutations in the CSBT gene.
Lafora body epilepsy (PME-2) results from mutations in EPM2A or NHLRC1 genes.
Neuronal ceroid lipofuscinosis and mitochondrial disorders are other considerations.
Essential myoclonus is a benign familial condition, often alcohol sensitive, linked to epsilon-sarcoglycan gene mutations.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · MYOCLONUS

items.title: Management of Drug-Induced Myoclonus
items.content: Drug-induced myoclonus can be managed by addressing the underlying condition or removing the offending agent. Pharmacologic therapy often involves GABAergic agents, which may lead to significant clinical improvement in chronic cases with a cortical origin.
keyPoints: GABAergic agents used include valproic acid (800–3000 mg/d), piracetam (8–20 g/d), clonazepam (2–15 mg/d), levetiracetam (1000–3000 mg/d), and primidone (500–1000 mg/d).
5-hydroxytryptophan with carbodopa may be useful in postanoxic myoclonus.
DBS is effective for myoclonus-dystonia, while botulinum toxin can help focal myoclonus and hemifacial spasm.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · Myoclonus | DRUG-INDUCED MOVEMENT DISORDERS | ACUTE

items.title: Acute Hyperkinetic Drug Reactions: Dystonia
items.content: Dystonia is the most common acute hyperkinetic drug reaction, often presenting as generalized in children and focal in adults. It can develop rapidly after exposure to certain drugs and is treatable with anticholinergics, diphenhydramine, benzodiazepines, or dopamine agonists.
keyPoints: Dystonia may present as blepharospasm, torticollis, or OMD in adults.
Treatment includes parenteral administration of benztropine, diphenhydramine, lorazepam, clonazepam, diazepam, or dopamine agonists.
Differentiate from seizures: no loss of consciousness, automatisms, EEG abnormalities, or postictal features.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · Myoclonus | DRUG-INDUCED MOVEMENT DISORDERS | ACUTE

items.title: Akathisia: Identification and Management
items.content: Akathisia is a motor restlessness often associated with neuroleptic drugs, presenting within 2 weeks of therapy initiation. It can be managed by dose reduction or discontinuation of the offending agent, supplemented by benzodiazepines, anticholinergics, beta blockers, or dopamine agonists.
keyPoints: Most common reaction in subacute category
Associated with neuroleptic drugs and other agents like calcium channel blockers
Onset within 2 weeks of initiating therapy
Management: Lower dose or remove agent; use benzodiazepines, anticholinergics, beta blockers, dopamine agonists if needed
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · SUBACUTE | TARDIVE SYNDROMES (part 1)

items.title: Tardive Dyskinesia: Risk Factors and Management
items.content: Tardive dyskinesias develop months to years after neuroleptic use, presenting with choreiform movements. Risk factors include elderly age, female gender, and underlying cerebral dysfunction. Treatment involves tapering the offending agent or switching to atypical antipsychotics.
keyPoints: Develops months to years post-neuroleptic initiation
Choreiform movements involving mouth, lips, tongue; may affect trunk, limbs, respiratory muscles
One-third remit within 3 months of stopping the drug
Risk factors: Elderly, females, underlying cerebral dysfunction
Management: Taper and withdraw offending agent; switch to atypical antipsychotics if necessary
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · SUBACUTE | TARDIVE SYNDROMES (part 1)

items.title: Treatment Options for Tardive Dyskinesia
items.content: Tetrabenazine and valbenazine are VMAT-2 inhibitors used to treat tardive dyskinesia, with valbenazine approved at 80 mg/d. Deuterated tetrabenazine offers a longer half-life with fewer side effects. Other treatments include valproic acid, anticholinergics, botulinum toxin, baclofen, and clonazepam.
keyPoints: Tetrabenazine: VMAT-2 inhibitor; short-acting; may worsen parkinsonian features
Valbenazine: Approved at 80 mg/d; associated with sleepiness and QT prolongation
Deuterated tetrabenazine: Longer half-life, lower $ C_{max} $, reduced risk of side effects
Other treatments: Valproic acid (750–3000 mg/d), anticholinergics, botulinum toxin, baclofen (40–80 mg/d), clonazepam (1–8 mg/d)
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · SUBACUTE | TARDIVE SYNDROMES (part 1)

items.title: Neuroleptic Malignant Syndrome: Recognition and Management
items.content: NMS is a serious condition associated with neuroleptic medications, characterized by muscle rigidity, hyperthermia, altered mental status, tachycardia, labile blood pressure, renal failure, and elevated creatine kinase levels. It typically develops within days to weeks of initiating or abruptly withdrawing dopaminergic drugs.
keyPoints: Symptoms: Muscle rigidity, hyperthermia, altered mental status, tachycardia, labile BP, renal failure
Lab finding: Markedly elevated creatine kinase levels
Management: Immediate cessation of antipsychotic, introduction of dopaminergic agents (e.g., dopamine agonist or levodopa), dantrolene, benzodiazepines
Severe cases may require rotigotine patch or apomorphine/levodopa infusion pump
Supportive measures: Temperature control, hydration, electrolyte replacement, renal function and BP management
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · TARDIVE SYNDROMES (part 2)

items.title: Serotonin Syndrome: Identification and Treatment
items.content: Serotonin syndrome is a potentially fatal condition induced by drugs with serotonin-like activity or those that block serotonin reuptake. It presents with confusion, hyperthermia, tachycardia, coma, rigidity, ataxia, tremor, and often prominent myoclonus.
keyPoints: Symptoms: Confusion, hyperthermia, tachycardia, coma, rigidity, ataxia, tremor, prominent myoclonus
Differential from NMS: Prominent myoclonus in serotonin syndrome
Management: Propranolol, diazepam, diphenhydramine, chlorpromazine, cyproheptadine
Supportive measures are also important
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · TARDIVE SYNDROMES (part 2)

items.title: Characteristics and Management of Paroxysmal Kinesigenic Dyskinesia (PKD)
items.content: PKD is characterized by brief, self-limited attacks triggered by sudden movement or unexpected stimuli. It often presents with mixed hyperkinetic movements such as dystonia, ballismus, and chorea. PKD typically manifests in the first or second decade of life, predominantly affects males (4:1), and may have a familial autosomal dominant inheritance pattern linked to PRRT2 gene mutations.
keyPoints: PKD attacks are triggered by sudden movement or unexpected stimuli such as sound or light.
Attacks last seconds to minutes and can recur several times daily.
Commonly affects males with a 4:1 ratio, onset in the first or second decade of life.
~70% report sensory symptoms preceding attacks by milliseconds.
Treatment with low-dose anticonvulsants like carbamazepine or phenytoin is effective in ~80% of patients.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · PAROXYSMAL DYSKINESIAS

items.title: Understanding Paroxysmal Nonkinesigenic Dyskinesia (PNKD)
items.content: PNKD involves longer-duration attacks triggered by factors like alcohol, caffeine, stress, or fatigue. It is inherited as an autosomal dominant condition with high (~80%) but incomplete penetrance and is associated with a mutation in the PNKD gene.
keyPoints: Attacks last minutes to hours, occurring one to three times per day.
Inherited as autosomal dominant with ~80% penetrance.
Treatment focuses on eliminating precipitating factors; tetrabenazine, neuroleptics, and benzodiazepines may be helpful.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · PAROXYSMAL DYSKINESIAS

items.title: Features of Paroxysmal Exertion-Induced Dyskinesia (PED)
items.content: PED is characterized by chorea, athetosis, and dystonia in exercised body regions, often after prolonged physical activity. It can be familial and associated with other conditions like epilepsy or migraine. The SLC2A1 gene mutation linked to GLUT1 deficiency syndrome may cause PED.
keyPoints: Attacks occur post-prolonged exercise, lasting minutes to an hour.
Frequently familial; associated with epilepsy, hemolytic anemia, and migraine.
Treatment includes avoiding prolonged exercise; ketogenic diet may be effective.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · PAROXYSMAL DYSKINESIAS

items.title: Diagnostic Criteria and Prevalence of Restless Legs Syndrome
items.content: Restless legs syndrome (RLS) is a neurologic disorder with core symptoms including an urge to move the legs, often accompanied by unpleasant sensations that worsen during rest and improve with movement. It affects approximately 10% of adults globally but is rare in Asians.
keyPoints: Affects ~10% of adult population
Rare in Asians
Core symptoms: urge to move, unpleasant sensation, worsening with rest, relief by movement, worse in evening/night
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · RESTLESS LEGS SYNDROME

items.title: Association and Pathogenesis of Restless Legs Syndrome
items.content: RLS is often associated with periodic leg movements during sleep (PLMs), depression, anxiety, and hypertension. It has a genetic component with over 150 variants identified in genome association studies, though no causative gene for primary RLS has been pinpointed.
keyPoints: ~80% of patients have PLMs
Associated with depression, anxiety, hypertension
>150 genetic variants linked to RLS risk (PTPRD, BTBD9, MEIS1 genes)
No causative gene identified
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · RESTLESS LEGS SYNDROME

items.title: Management and Treatment Options for Restless Legs Syndrome
items.content: Initial management of RLS includes improving sleep hygiene. For persistent symptoms, low doses of dopamine agonists such as pramipexole (0.25–0.5 mg), ropinirole (1–2 mg), or rotigotine patch (2–3 mg) are effective. Tonic motor activation is a nonpharmacologic treatment option for drug-resistant cases.
keyPoints: Improve sleep hygiene first
Dopamine agonists: pramipexole 0.25–0.5 mg, ropinirole 1–2 mg, rotigotine patch 2–3 mg
Tonic motor activation (TOMAC) as nonpharmacologic treatment
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · RESTLESS LEGS SYNDROME

items.title: Wilson's Disease: A Must-Consider in Young Patients with Movement Disorders
items.content: Wilson's disease should be considered when diagnosing movement disorders, especially if they arise in the first decades of life. It is characterized by neurologic manifestations such as tremor, rigidity, and dystonia.
keyPoints: Caused by mutations in the ATP7B gene affecting copper metabolism.
Prevalence: ~1 in 30,000; mutation carrier frequency: 1 in 90.

Neurologic onset typically manifests in the second decade with tremor (often upper limbs, bilateral, asymmetric), rigidity, and dystonia. Tremors can be intention or rest-based, sometimes taking on a wing-beating characteristic.
Kayser-Fleischer rings are present in virtually all patients with neurologic features and 80% of those with hepatic presentations.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · WILSON'S DISEASE (SEE ALSO CHAP. 427)

items.title: Diagnostic Approach to Wilson's Disease
items.content: Diagnosis of Wilson's disease involves a combination of clinical features, laboratory tests, imaging, and genetic testing.
keyPoints: Low blood copper and ceruloplasmin levels with high urinary copper may be present but are not definitive.
Brain MRI may show generalized brain atrophy and signal hypointensity in the caudate head, putamen, globus pallidus, substantia nigra, and red nucleus on T2-weighted scans (~50% of cases).
Liver biopsy demonstrating high copper levels and genetic testing are the gold standard for diagnosis.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · WILSON'S DISEASE (SEE ALSO CHAP. 427)

items.title: Treatment Strategies for Wilson's Disease
items.content: Effective treatment of Wilson's disease focuses on reducing tissue copper levels and preventing reaccumulation, with several therapeutic options available.
keyPoints: Penicillamine is frequently used to increase copper excretion but may initially worsen symptoms; side effects can be mitigated by co-administration of pyridoxine.
Tetrathiomolybdate blocks copper absorption and serves as an alternative to penicillamine.
Trientine tetrahydrochloride and zinc are useful for maintenance therapy.
Early treatment can reverse neurologic features in most patients, though progression may occur with hepatocerebral disease.
Kayser-Fleischer rings typically decrease after 3–6 months and disappear by 2 years.
Adherence to maintenance therapy is crucial; liver transplant may be necessary for advanced hepatic disease.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · WILSON'S DISEASE (SEE ALSO CHAP. 427)

items.title: Recognizing Pantothenate Kinase-Associated Neurodegeneration (PKAN)
items.content: Pantothenate kinase-associated neurodegeneration, formerly known as Hallervorden-Spatz disease, is the most common form of NBIA. It results from a mutation in the PANK2 gene and accounts for approximately 50% of NBIA cases. The disorder typically presents in early childhood with dystonia, parkinsonism, and spasticity.
keyPoints: Pantothenate kinase-associated neurodegeneration (PKAN) is caused by mutations in the PANK2 gene.
~50% of NBIA cases are due to PKAN.
Onset is usually in early childhood.
Clinical features include dystonia, parkinsonism, and spasticity.
MRI shows a characteristic 'eye of the tiger' sign on T2-weighted scans.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · NEURODEGENERATION WITH BRAIN IRON ACCUMULATION

items.title: Differential Diagnosis for Basal Ganglia Iron Accumulation
items.content: While NBIA is characterized by iron accumulation in the basal ganglia, it's crucial to differentiate it from other conditions where similar findings may occur. Not all cases of iron accumulation in the basal ganglia are due to NBIA; some may be normal or secondary to other processes.
keyPoints: NBIA involves inherited disorders with brain iron accumulation.
Iron accumulation can also be a nonspecific secondary consequence.
Multiple genes (e.g., PLA2G6, C19orf12, FA2H) are associated with NBIA beyond PANK2.
Caution is needed to avoid misdiagnosing other causes of basal ganglia iron accumulation as NBIA.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · NEURODEGENERATION WITH BRAIN IRON ACCUMULATION

items.question: Which medication has been approved for the treatment of tardive dyskinesia based on double-blind trials?
items.explanation: Valbenazine has been approved for the treatment of tardive dyskinesia based on efficacy in double-blind trials.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · SUBACUTE | TARDIVE SYNDROMES (part 1)

items.question: Which of the following is a characteristic feature that distinguishes serotonin syndrome from neuroleptic malignant syndrome?
items.explanation: Myoclonus is often a prominent feature of serotonin syndrome, unlike neuroleptic malignant syndrome.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · TARDIVE SYNDROMES (part 2)

items.question: Which medication is NOT typically used in the management of serotonin syndrome?
items.explanation: Levodopa is not used for managing serotonin syndrome; it's associated with dyskinetic movements in Parkinson's disease.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · TARDIVE SYNDROMES (part 2)

items.question: Which of the following drugs can induce a neuroleptic malignant syndrome?
items.explanation: Neuroleptic medications can be associated with neuroleptic malignant syndrome.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · TARDIVE SYNDROMES (part 2)

items.question: Which gene mutation is most commonly associated with paroxysmal kinesigenic dyskinesia (PKD)?
items.explanation: Mutations in the proline-rich transmembrane protein 2 (PRRT2) gene are most commonly associated with PKD.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · PAROXYSMAL DYSKINESIAS

items.question: What is a common trigger for attacks of paroxysmal kinesigenic dyskinesia?
items.explanation: PKD attacks are commonly triggered by sudden movements.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · PAROXYSMAL DYSKINESIAS

items.question: Which treatment is typically effective for PKD?
items.explanation: Low-dose anticonvulsant therapy such as carbamazepine or phenytoin is advised for PKD.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · PAROXYSMAL DYSKINESIAS

items.question: Which gene mutation has been identified in several families with paroxysmal nonkinesigenic dyskinesia (PNKD)?
items.explanation: A missense mutation in the myofibrillogenesis regulator (PNKD) gene has been identified in several families with PNKD.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · PAROXYSMAL DYSKINESIAS

items.question: Which of the following is NOT one of the four core symptoms required for diagnosing Restless Legs Syndrome (RLS)?
options: An urge to move the legs usually caused or accompanied by an unpleasant sensation in the legs.
Symptoms that begin or worsen with rest and are partially relieved by movement.
Worsening of symptoms during the evening or night.
Presence of involuntary movements lasting more than a few seconds.
items.explanation: Involuntary movements in RLS usually last no more than a few seconds, not longer.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · RESTLESS LEGS SYNDROME

items.question: Which of the following is commonly associated with Restless Legs Syndrome (RLS)?
options: No association with depression or anxiety.
items.explanation: ~80% of patients with RLS have periodic leg movements (PLMs) during sleep.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · RESTLESS LEGS SYNDROME

items.question: What is a major cause of poor-quality sleep in individuals with Restless Legs Syndrome (RLS)?
options: Restlessness and periodic leg movements during sleep.
items.explanation: The restlessness and PLMs are a major cause of sleep disturbance in RLS patients.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · RESTLESS LEGS SYNDROME

items.question: Which treatment is reported to be effective for RLS that has not responded to drug therapy?
items.explanation: TOMAC involves electrical stimulation of the peroneal nerves and has been approved for RLS unresponsive to drugs.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · RESTLESS LEGS SYNDROME

items.question: Which gene mutation is responsible for Wilson's disease?
items.explanation: Wilson's disease is caused by mutations in the ATP7B gene.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · WILSON'S DISEASE (SEE ALSO CHAP. 427)

items.question: What are Kayser-Fleischer rings composed of?
items.explanation: Kayser-Fleischer rings represent the deposition of copper in Descemet's membrane around the cornea.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · WILSON'S DISEASE (SEE ALSO CHAP. 427)

items.question: Which symptom is most characteristic of neurologic onset in Wilson's disease?
items.explanation: Neurologic onset usually manifests with a bilateral, asymmetric tremor in the upper limbs.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · WILSON'S DISEASE (SEE ALSO CHAP. 427)

items.question: What is considered the gold standard for diagnosing Wilson's disease?
options: Liver biopsy with copper level demonstration and genetic testing
items.explanation: Diagnosis of Wilson's disease relies on liver biopsy showing high copper levels and genetic testing.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · WILSON'S DISEASE (SEE ALSO CHAP. 427)

items.question: Which gene mutation is most commonly associated with Pantothenate kinase-associated neurodegeneration (PKAN)?
items.explanation: PKAN is caused by a mutation in the PANK2 gene.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · NEURODEGENERATION WITH BRAIN IRON ACCUMULATION

items.question: What MRI finding is characteristic of PKAN?
options: Eye of the tiger sign in the globus pallidus
items.explanation: The 'eye of the tiger' sign on T2-weighted MRI scans is characteristic of PKAN.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · NEURODEGENERATION WITH BRAIN IRON ACCUMULATION

items.question: Which clinical feature is NOT typically associated with NBIA disorders?
items.explanation: Peripheral neuropathy is not a typical feature of NBIA disorders.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · NEURODEGENERATION WITH BRAIN IRON ACCUMULATION

items.subtopic: Tremor, Chorea, and Other Movement Disorders | HYPERKINETIC MOVEMENT DISORDERS | CLINICAL FEATURES
items.statement: Cerebellar dysfunction is characterized by a kinetic tremor.
items.explanation: Cerebellar dysfunction typically presents with a kinetic tremor.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · Tremor, Chorea, and Other Movement Disorders | HYPERKINETIC MOVEMENT DISORDERS | CLINICAL FEATURES

items.subtopic: Tremor, Chorea, and Other Movement Disorders | HYPERKINETIC MOVEMENT DISORDERS | CLINICAL FEATURES
items.statement: Hyperkinetic movement disorders are characterized by involuntary movements accompanied by weakness.
items.explanation: Hyperkinetic movement disorders involve involuntary movements unaccompanied by weakness.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · Tremor, Chorea, and Other Movement Disorders | HYPERKINETIC MOVEMENT DISORDERS | CLINICAL FEATURES

items.statement: Essential tremor predominantly affects the lower extremities.
items.explanation: Essential tremor predominantly affects the upper extremities.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · ESSENTIAL TREMOR

items.statement: Parkinson's disease (PD) is typically associated with a postural or action tremor.
items.explanation: Parkinson's disease tends to be present primarily at rest, not as a postural or action tremor.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · ESSENTIAL TREMOR

items.statement: Approximately 50% of essential tremor (ET) patients have a positive family history with an autosomal dominant pattern.
items.explanation: Approximately 50% of ET patients do have a positive family history with an autosomal dominant pattern.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · ETIOLOGY AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY | TREATMENT

items.statement: Primidone is started at high doses to avoid sedation, nausea, and dizziness.
items.explanation: Primidone should be started at low doses (12.5 mg) and gradually increased to avoid side effects.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · ETIOLOGY AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY | TREATMENT

items.statement: The frequency of dystonia is estimated to be about 30 per 100,000.
items.explanation: The passage states the frequency of dystonia is estimated at about 30 per 100,000.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · CLINICAL FEATURES

items.statement: Dystonia can only be classified based on clinical features.
items.explanation: Dystonia can be classified both clinically and etiologically.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · CLINICAL FEATURES

items.statement: Focal hand or leg dystonia is more common than cervical dystonia.
items.explanation: Cervical dystonia (~40%) is much more common than focal hand or leg dystonia (~10%).
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · ISOLATED DYSTONIAS (part 1)

items.statement: Mutations in the TOR1A gene are recessively inherited.
items.explanation: The TOR1A mutation is a dominant inheritance pattern.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · ISOLATED DYSTONIAS (part 1)

items.statement: Pathogenic ANO3 mutations are confirmed by their presence exclusively in patients with dystonia.
items.explanation: ANO3 mutations can be found in healthy individuals, though they have a pathogenic role in some families.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · ISOLATED DYSTONIAS (part 2)

items.statement: The majority of PRKRA mutation carriers develop isolated dystonia without laryngeal involvement.
items.explanation: PRKRA mutation carriers typically develop generalized dystonia, often with laryngeal involvement.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · ISOLATED DYSTONIAS (part 2)

items.statement: Dopa-responsive dystonia is characterized by a lack of response to levodopa therapy.
items.explanation: Dopa-responsive dystonia dramatically responds to low doses of levodopa.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · COMBINED DYSTONIAS (part 1)

items.statement: SGCE mutations in myoclonic-dystonia are inherited from the father due to maternal imprinting.
items.explanation: SGCE mutations manifest when inherited from the father because of maternal imprinting.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · COMBINED DYSTONIAS (part 1)

items.statement: Dystonia can develop following peripheral nerve injury and be associated with features of complex regional pain syndrome.
items.explanation: The passage states that dystonia can rarely develop after peripheral nerve injury, sometimes presenting with complex regional pain syndrome.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · COMBINED DYSTONIAS (part 2) | PATHOPHYSIOLOGY OF DYSTONIA

items.statement: A specific pathology has been consistently identified that underlies dystonia.
items.explanation: The passage mentions that no specific pathology has been consistently identified underlying dystonia.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · COMBINED DYSTONIAS (part 2) | PATHOPHYSIOLOGY OF DYSTONIA

items.statement: Botulinum toxin treatment for dystonia is associated with systemic side effects.
items.explanation: No systemic side effects are encountered with the doses typically used for botulinum toxin.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · Dystonia

items.statement: Deep brain stimulation (DBS) of the pallidum is effective in treating both hereditary and nonhereditary generalized dystonia.
items.explanation: DBS can provide dramatic benefits for various forms of hereditary and nonhereditary generalized dystonia.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · Dystonia

items.statement: Genetic testing for Huntington's disease does not require counseling.
items.explanation: Genetic testing must be performed in conjunction with trained counselors due to the potential psychological impact.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · HUNTINGTON'S DISEASE

items.statement: Depression and suicidal tendencies can be prominent features of Huntington's disease.
items.explanation: Behavioral disturbances in HD patients may include depression with suicidal tendencies, aggressive behavior, and psychosis.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · HUNTINGTON'S DISEASE

items.statement: Expansion of repeat length tends to occur more frequently in females.
items.explanation: Repeat expansion tends to occur particularly in males, not females.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · ETIOLOGY

items.statement: New-onset gene expansion in patients with no family history is common.
items.explanation: New-onset gene expansion in patients with no family history is rare.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · ETIOLOGY

items.statement: Tetrabenazine and valbenazine are both dopamine-blocking agents approved for treating choreiform movements in Huntington's disease.
items.explanation: Both tetrabenazine and valbenazine are mentioned as dopamine-blocking agents approved to treat the choreiform movements.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · Huntington's Disease (part 1)

items.statement: Neuroleptics are recommended as first-line treatment for Huntington's disease chorea.
items.explanation: Neuroleptics are generally not recommended due to their potential to induce other movement disorders.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · Huntington's Disease (part 1)

items.statement: Sydenham's chorea is typically seen in adulthood.
items.explanation: Sydenham's chorea is more common in childhood, particularly ages 5–15 years.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · Huntington's Disease (part 2) | ☐ OTHER CHOREAS

items.statement: Chorea-acanthocytosis can be associated with self-mutilating behavior.
items.explanation: Chorea-acanthocytosis is characterized by severe chorea that can include self-mutilating behavior.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · Huntington's Disease (part 2) | ☐ OTHER CHOREAS

items.statement: Hemibalismus can resolve spontaneously after weeks or months.
items.explanation: Hemibalismus is usually self-limiting and tends to resolve spontaneously.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · BALLISM/HEMIBALLISMUS | TICS | TOURETTE'S SYNDROME

items.statement: Phonic tics can only be simple, such as grunting.
items.explanation: Phonic tics can also be complex, including echolalia and coprolalia.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · BALLISM/HEMIBALLISMUS | TICS | TOURETTE'S SYNDROME

items.statement: Behavioral therapies have been formally tested and proven effective for tic control.
items.explanation: Behavioral and speech therapies are occasionally employed but have not been formally tested.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · Tics and Tourette's Syndrome

items.statement: ADHD medications such as methylphenidate can exacerbate tics.
items.explanation: Methylphenidate is sometimes used to increase attention but may also exacerbate tics.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · Tics and Tourette's Syndrome

items.statement: Myoclonic jerks are typically repetitive and suppressible.
items.explanation: Myoclonic jerks differ from tics in that they are typically not repetitive, are not suppressible.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · MYOCLONUS

items.statement: Essential myoclonus is a relatively benign familial condition characterized by multifocal movements.
items.explanation: Essential myoclonus is indeed a relatively benign familial condition with multifocal, very brief, lightning-like movements.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · MYOCLONUS

items.statement: Anticholinergics are effective anti-parkinsonian agents for treating neuroleptic-induced parkinsonism.
items.explanation: Anticholinergics are not effective anti-parkinsonian agents and may increase the risk of developing a tardive syndrome.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · Myoclonus | DRUG-INDUCED MOVEMENT DISORDERS | ACUTE

items.statement: The acute onset of severe spasms due to drug reaction can be confused with seizures.
items.explanation: Severe spasms from an acute drug reaction may resemble seizures but lack typical seizure features like loss of consciousness.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · Myoclonus | DRUG-INDUCED MOVEMENT DISORDERS | ACUTE

items.statement: Tardive dystonia frequently resolves after stopping medication.
items.explanation: Tardive dystonia frequently persists despite stopping medication.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · SUBACUTE | TARDIVE SYNDROMES (part 1)

items.statement: Abrupt cessation of neuroleptic agents is recommended to treat tardive dyskinesia.
items.explanation: Abrupt cessation should be avoided because it can induce worsening of symptoms.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · SUBACUTE | TARDIVE SYNDROMES (part 1)

items.statement: Abrupt withdrawal of dopaminergic medications in PD patients can precipitate neuroleptic malignant syndrome.
items.explanation: Abrupt withdrawal of dopaminergic medications can indeed precipitate NMS.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · TARDIVE SYNDROMES (part 2)

items.statement: Serotonin syndrome is characterized by muscle rigidity and altered mental status, but not hyperthermia.
items.explanation: Serotonin syndrome includes hyperthermia as one of its characteristic features.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · TARDIVE SYNDROMES (part 2)

items.statement: Paroxysmal exertion-induced dyskinesia is triggered by sudden movement.
items.explanation: PED is induced by prolonged exercise, not sudden movement.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · PAROXYSMAL DYSKINESIAS

items.statement: Anticonvulsant drugs are typically effective in treating paroxysmal nonkinesigenic dyskinesia.
items.explanation: Most patients with PNKD do not benefit from anticonvulsant drugs.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · PAROXYSMAL DYSKINESIAS

items.statement: Restless Legs Syndrome (RLS) is more common in Asians.
items.explanation: RLS is rare in Asians.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · RESTLESS LEGS SYNDROME

items.statement: Primary Restless Legs Syndrome often has a strong genetic component.
items.explanation: Primary RLS is often familial with a strong genetic component.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · RESTLESS LEGS SYNDROME

items.statement: Kayser-Fleischer rings are seen in all patients with hepatic presentations of Wilson's disease.
items.explanation: KF rings are seen in 80% of those with hepatic presentations, not all.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · WILSON'S DISEASE (SEE ALSO CHAP. 427)

items.statement: Effective treatment can reverse neurologic features if started early in Wilson's disease.
items.explanation: Early treatment of Wilson's disease can indeed reverse the neurologic features.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · WILSON'S DISEASE (SEE ALSO CHAP. 427)

items.statement: Iron accumulation in the basal ganglia always indicates an NBIA disorder.
items.explanation: Not all cases of iron accumulation in the basal ganglia represent NBIA; it can be a nonspecific secondary consequence.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · NEURODEGENERATION WITH BRAIN IRON ACCUMULATION

items.statement: Cognitive disorders and cerebellar dysfunction may occur in NBIA.
items.explanation: NBIA can manifest with cognitive disorders and cerebellar dysfunction.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · NEURODEGENERATION WITH BRAIN IRON ACCUMULATION

items.assertion: DYT-TOR1A dystonia typically responds well to deep brain stimulation.
items.reason: The majority of patients with DYT-TOR1A and DYTKMT2B respond positively to DBS.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · ISOLATED DYSTONIAS (part 1)

items.assertion: Mutations in THAP1 gene lead to early-onset generalized dystonia.
items.reason: THAP1 mutations manifest with adolescent-onset dystonia with mixed phenotype.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; THAP1 mutations typically cause adolescent-onset dystonia.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · ISOLATED DYSTONIAS (part 1)

items.assertion: The majority of AOPEP gene mutation carriers have an onset in the hands and progress to generalized dystonia.
items.reason: Carriers of recessively inherited, biallelic pathogenic variants in AOPEP typically show frequent onset in the hands with progression to isolated, generalized dystonia.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · ISOLATED DYSTONIAS (part 2)

items.assertion: X-linked dystonia-parkinsonism is found exclusively in patients of Filipino origin.
items.reason: A founder effect with a retrotransposon insertion in the TAF1 gene causes this condition.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason explains why X-linked dystonia-parkinsonism is found exclusively in Filipino patients.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · COMBINED DYSTONIAS (part 1)

items.assertion: Mutations in ATP1A3 gene result in a lifelong response to levodopa therapy.
items.reason: The dopamine neuronal network is anatomically preserved, avoiding dyskinesia with chronic treatment.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; it's mutations in GCH1 that result in a lifelong response to levodopa without dyskinesia.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · COMBINED DYSTONIAS (part 1)

items.assertion: Neuroleptics are typically not recommended in the treatment of dystonia.
items.reason: They have the potential to induce parkinsonism and other movement disorders, including tardive dystonia.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true; neuroleptics can induce dystonia and are not recommended.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · Dystonia

items.assertion: Peripheral procedures such as rhizotomy and myotomy are commonly used in current treatment of cervical dystonia.
items.reason: These procedures were historically employed but are now rarely used.
items.explanation: The assertion is false, while the reason is true; these procedures are rarely used now.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · Dystonia

items.assertion: The majority of Huntington's disease patients progress to dementia.
items.reason: HD patients eventually develop behavioral and cognitive disturbances.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why most HD patients progress to dementia.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · HUNTINGTON'S DISEASE

items.assertion: The Westphal variant of Huntington's disease presents with chorea.
items.reason: In younger patients, HD can present as an akinetic-rigid parkinsonian syndrome known as the Westphal variant.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because the Westphal variant presents with a parkinsonian syndrome, not chorea.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · HUNTINGTON'S DISEASE

items.assertion: Deuterated tetrabenazine can be administered in higher doses with potentially superior clinical benefits.
items.reason: Deuteration interferes with the metabolism of tetrabenazine and avoids high maximum concentration, which is thought to contribute to adverse effects.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why deuterated tetrabenazine can be administered in higher doses.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · Huntington's Disease (part 1)

items.assertion: Clinical trials of an antisense oligonucleotide for Huntington's disease were terminated early due to observed clinical benefits.
items.reason: The study was terminated early because no clinical benefit was detected.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; the trial was terminated early due to lack of clinical benefit, not because of observed benefits.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · Huntington's Disease (part 1)

items.assertion: Chorea-acanthocytosis is an autosomal recessive disorder.
items.reason: It is characterized by mutations in the VPS13A gene encoding chorein.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why it's considered autosomal recessive.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · Huntington's Disease (part 2) | ☐ OTHER CHOREAS

items.assertion: Chorea is always associated with Huntington's disease.
items.reason: Chorea can be seen in various other disorders, including genetic mutations and systemic diseases.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because chorea can occur in many conditions besides Huntington's disease.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · Huntington's Disease (part 2) | ☐ OTHER CHOREAS

items.assertion: Botulinum toxin is used in the treatment of focal myoclonus.
items.reason: Botulinum toxin has been successfully used for some patients with focal myoclonus, palatal myoclonus, and hemifacial spasm.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why botulinum toxin is used in these cases.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · Myoclonus | DRUG-INDUCED MOVEMENT DISORDERS | ACUTE

items.assertion: Younger patients have a lower risk of developing neuroleptic-induced tardive dyskinesia.
items.reason: The elderly, females, and those with underlying organic cerebral dysfunction are at greater risk.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · SUBACUTE | TARDIVE SYNDROMES (part 1)

items.assertion: Tetrabenazine is a long-acting treatment for tardive dyskinesia.
items.reason: It blocks storage of dopamine and has been used to treat TD.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because tetrabenazine is short-acting, though the reason is true.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · SUBACUTE | TARDIVE SYNDROMES (part 1)

items.assertion: Paroxysmal kinesigenic dyskinesia (PKD) is more common in females.
items.reason: PKD has a male predominance with a ratio of 4:1.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; PKD is more frequent in males.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · PAROXYSMAL DYSKINESIAS

items.assertion: A ketogenic diet may be an effective therapeutic option for paroxysmal exertion-induced dyskinesia.
items.reason: Most medications are typically not effective for PED, and a ketogenic diet can help manage symptoms.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is the correct explanation.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · PAROXYSMAL DYSKINESIAS

items.assertion: Restless Legs Syndrome (RLS) can cause significant morbidity.
items.reason: The symptoms of RLS, such as restlessness and periodic leg movements during sleep, lead to poor-quality sleep and daytime sleepiness.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason explains why RLS can cause significant morbidity.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · RESTLESS LEGS SYNDROME

items.assertion: Iron supplementation is a primary treatment for all cases of Restless Legs Syndrome (RLS).
items.reason: Abnormalities in iron metabolism are associated with RLS, and low iron can result in reduced dopamine levels.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because not all cases require iron supplementation; the reason is true regarding the association of iron abnormalities.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · RESTLESS LEGS SYNDROME

items.assertion: Penicillamine is used to increase copper excretion in Wilson's disease.
items.reason: It may lead to a worsening of symptoms initially during therapy.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the worsening of symptoms does not explain why penicillamine increases copper excretion.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · WILSON'S DISEASE (SEE ALSO CHAP. 427)

items.assertion: Gene therapy for Wilson's disease involves infusing a working copy of the ATP7B gene.
items.reason: This approach is already clinically approved and widely used in treatment.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, but the reason is false as this approach is still under clinical investigation.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · WILSON'S DISEASE (SEE ALSO CHAP. 427)

items.assertion: PKAN is the most common form of NBIA.
items.reason: It accounts for approximately 50% of NBIA cases due to mutations in the PANK2 gene.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why PKAN is the most common form.
items.reference: Tremor Chorea and Other Movement Disorders · NEURODEGENERATION WITH BRAIN IRON ACCUMULATION
